CURRENT AFFAIRS.

A few weeks ago we referred to the vacancy then
existing for a buyer in the wholesale firm_ of Messrs.
Crowe, Wilson and Co., Litd., of Lower Bridge Street.
The business of that firm is mainly in the '* South and
West,”” where the terrible Papists are in the greab

ajority. The firm employs eleven buyers, of whom

ine are non-Catholic and two are Catholic; and of six
ravellers only two are Catholics. There was a vacancy
or & buyer as one of the Protestant buyers left. In
previous reference to the matter we remarked :—-
owe, Wilson and Co., Ltd., live chiefly on the
m of the terrible  South and West,” and we will
xious o watch if the vacancy for a buyer will be

y that firm to  prove their tolerance ' in just a

y by reducing the disparity in the * colour * of
" Well, the vacancy has since been filled,
we Ison and Co., Ltd., do not appear to

i‘? ed their tolerance,’” as the vacancy has

been filled by a Protestant brought in from the outside.
In Belfast thousands of Catholics have been driven
from their work by Orange terrorism and lawlessness.
If Crowe, Wilson and Co. were as Catholic in its prin-
cipal staff as it is non-Catholic how much business
would it be allowed to do among the ‘‘ lambs’’ and de-
fenders of ** civil and religious liberty’” in Belfast?

We hear that Catholic medical men in Meath have
been passed over by the local section of Lady Aber-
deen’s Women’s National Health Association. In
connection with the Tuberculosis Section of the Insur-
ance Act the W.N.H.A. are establishing a central
Tuberculosis Dispensary in Navan. This dispensary
is not yet completed, in fact the work of construction
has only begun, but it is rather previous, as the Ame-
rican might put it, that the medical officer has already
been selected if not actually elected. There was no
advertisement, no open competition, and by a pure
stroke of chance, if we may put it that nice way, the
selected happens to be one of the ‘“ Saved.”” The job,
we understand, is worth about £500 a year, and accord-
ing to our information there has been no advertisement
and no real election. Suvely in the interests of the
patients the best possible man in the circumstances
should be selected, and how can that be done satisfac-
torily but by throwing the post open to free competition
and let the position go to a vote. Was any Catholic
doctor in Meath approached about this matter? We
are given to understand that the answer to that ques-
tion would be in the negative.

The Belfast Chamber of Commerce is a ‘‘ non-poli-
tical ” body! It is certainly rather ‘‘ non-political ** in
the sense conveyed by that term when placed in quota-
tion marks. It is a bigoted and anti-Irish body, full
of pharisaical self-esteem, and bursting with phari-
saical indignation against the ‘‘South and West."” It
stands for ‘‘ civil and religious liberty "’ in a commu-
nity where its kind won’t allow thousands of Catholics
to earn their daily bread by the sweat of their brows,
and from which refugees, fleeing from Belfast in terror
of their lives, recently landed in Dublin. This ‘‘ non-
political > Chamber of Commerce went to Mr. Asquith
with their old bigot tale, and not a word of apology or
an expression of the shame that they ought to feel.for
the savagery of their rank and file, who forced men to
flee from Belfast to Dublin in terror, and forced thou-
sands of honest men to cease for a period from at-
tempting to earn their bread by hard and honest work.
The British Prime Minister had ‘ the weight ™ of
them, and treated them with a geniality, courtesy and
respect which must have been galling to their narrow
and sour little hearts. One of the ** grievances ™ of
these gentry is that ‘‘ with regard to the question of
the Gaelic language in the examination of public ap-
pointments they would also suffer.”’ There’s an Irish
spirit for youl Tn their insensate bigotry they dread
even the Irish language. Mr. Asquith asked them if
they would like a safeguard in the Bill against this
fear of theirs concerning the Irish language, and there
were cries of ‘‘ Yes, yes.” Irish Ireland had better
keep_a sharp eye on this matter, as we will not have
the Irish language legally penalised. The Irish lan-
guage must have free scope to grow according to the
vitality that is in it, and public bodies must be free
to make Irish a subject for examination, as they are
free to make arithmetic or geography such a subject.
The bigots, the  non-political ™ bigots, representing
the Belfagt Chamber of Commerce, very much showed
their hand, if it is not a bull to put it so, by gnashing
their teeth at the Trish language. The Irish language

is the nearest thing we have to what in normal coun-
tries is ““ the flag,”’ and one's attitude towards it—
apart from any knowledge of it—throws a sort of
national X-rays on a man.

The effort to anglicise even the sun in' Ireland is
not yet quite dead, and there is a Bill, which, we be-
lieve, has no chance of passing this session, before the
House of Lords. If people want uniformity let Eng-
land adopt Irish time if it likes! Of course the anti-
Irish ““Irish Times ' wants English time. But it
need not be ridiculous in its advocacy. ' Some ex-
treme adherents,’” it says, ‘‘of the ‘ Ireland a nation ’
theory, claim that, as a nation, Ireland has a right to
her own time.”” So ‘‘Ireland a nation’’ is only a
theory then! But what follows is more ridiculous.
““ This argument is not only sentimental, but falla-
cious. To suggest that, at a time when Treland’s pros-
perity is increasing by leaps and bounds, she should be
doomed for ever to be twenty-five minutes behind the
times is a sublime insult to our nationhood.”  This
is a specimen of the undigested twaddle that the ** Irish
Times’’ doles out to its readers. In the first part of
the argument ‘‘ Treland a nation '’ is sneered at: in
the latter part the element of nationality is laid under
contribution! And the contention about Ireland being
twenty-five minutes behind ‘‘ the times ~'—what does
““ the times '’ stand for? Is it Pigott’s * Times,” of
which the editor of Westmoreland Street ‘* Times '’ is
Dublin correspondent ? That contention is surely more
than ordinarily puerile, even for the columns of our
contemporary. Why, the United States of America are
L hours behind “ the times ** according to this line of
argument. Is it not a wonder that, being so far be-
hind ‘‘ the times,”’ they are able to exist at all? Those
Anglicisers who want even to John Bulldose the sun
in Ireland might at least endeavour to be less silly.

A contributor in another column writes interestingly
on a subject of importance and topical interest—he
< calls his article ‘“ Our Schools.”” Like everyone else,
o % his experience is naturally limited. We suppose most
8 articles at this period on such a question are more or
less puff articles, written more or less on the under-
standing—no advertisement no puff. The article we
s refer to, we need scarcely say, has no connection with
~ ads. nor puffs: it represents the individual views of a
2 very intellectual man.  What about girls’ schools?
" And what about the type that other leading colleges
turn out? Our contributor speaks of his own observa-
tion and experience, and that is inevitably limited.
Hé has written interestingly but by no means exhaus-
tively on a very topical and not altogether, shall we

say, non-thorny subject. Z

The Church of England’s Faithful Garrison in Ire-
land, whilst it is anti-Irish and would probably sneer
ab the sentiment of Ireland a nation, or at best main-
tain the two nation theory, and claim that it belonged
to the foreign section, still persists in calling itself ‘‘The
Church of Ireland.”’. The Standing Committee of the
General Synod of this partly political institution,
‘“drawn up as directed by the archbishops, bishops,
clergy and laity, in Special:General Synod assembled
in Dublin, 16th day of April, 1912,”" adopted an_anti-
Home Rule petition which was presented to'the Lords
by Lord Londonderry on the 27th June. The petition is

the usual mixture of anti-Trish bitters.  One of the
- signatories; in his capacity as one of the hon. secs., is
- no.other than Capt. R. Wade Thompson, the Pervert
‘Priests’ protector. Surely it may be said' of petitions—
shi ignatories and I'll tell you what you are.

PEARLS BEFORE SWINE.
Scene—A part of the Soubh.
(Enter two Protestents.)

1sb P.—The cool assurance of the Papists here.
Doth take my b[reubh ﬁwny.
2nd P.—They say, forsooth,
The scm,{crcg “ Saved *’ throughout the South and
‘West, -
Who find the Papists-tolerant and mild,
Should lift their voices in protesting speech
Against the horrid bigots of the_North,
Whene’er in fell ferocity they rise
And malke Belfast a pit of yelling fiends
To Rome's bread-seeking workers.
1st P.—The Tvish Papists do expect too much
If they imagine that the scattered ‘‘ Saved
Will from their mean tradition so depart
As to denounce their loyal kith and kin
In aught that doth affect the sacred cause
Of Protestant ascendancy.
2nd P.—If sourfaced aliens in the rebel South
Can live in peace among the Romish hordes,
Tt is no reason why they should so far
Torget their venom towards the native Gael
As pay a public tribute to the worth
Of Papist toleration in the South,
Or mark the savage contrast of the North,
Where law and order reign.
1st P.—Not likely.
The Papists may be models of sweet peace,
And all the civic virtues that do make
A nation flourish under native laws,
But never, never, must they so appear
Unto the jaundiced vision of the ‘‘ Saved,”’
Whose direst nightmare is a normal state
Where Papists could enjoy the common rights
Of people civilised.
2nd P.—Bebween ourselves
The Irish are too civilised for us,
And like un-Christian ruffians will not act
To suit our sordid policy howe’er
We egg them to reprisals.
1st P.—Alas! 'tis so.
We cannot make them bigots like ourselves
Do what we may. But hang them all,
We'll be defamers of the Romish breed
Unto the very last.
2nd P.—We will.
And any craven Protestant who speaks,
With disrespect of Ulster’s savage gang,
Will at our hands receive his just reward
Of boycott and exclusion.
1st P.—TLet him conceal his decent feelings well
Or else abide the issue.
2nd P.—We want no thinking Protestants.
1st P.—Thought is dangerous .
Unto the Union.
2nd P.—Hands oft Ulster
And Orange Yahooes,
1st P.—No Home Rule.
(Bxeunt.)

creants. The next hour a Catholic was taken prisoner
‘by im_n': other ruffians, 'his arms pinioned to his sides
and his head battered by the blows given by the four.
(A fifth now came up and felled the victim unconscious
by.tl_lq blow of an iron bar! Madly his distressed co-
re}xgmnists flocked to the gates, and in the rush escaped
with their lives only. At the Vigilance Committee
held later in St. Mary’s Hall, under the presidency of
Most Rev. Dr. Tohill, it was heartrending to see these
maimed, bruised workers, with heads bandaged and
faces disfigured, appearing to tell their pitiable stories
of the brutality meted out to them. How, it may be
asked, was their identity as Catholics known to their
assailants ?  An Orangeman passing to his work claps
the Catholic known to him on back or shoulder, with
‘How're ye?’ The saluted, scenting danger, remarks
to his chum on the job, ‘Hi, Jimmy. Anything on
me?’ ‘Aye! Chalk mark on yir back.” With that the
foreman approaching instantly summons ‘Jimmy’ to
follow him to the ‘shop’ a hundred yards off. = Once
there, a huried ¢ Run for your life! Never mind coat !’
And the race begins. The baffled human wolves, see-
ing their quarry escape, fly in pursuit—but the hunted
one has the few moments’ advantage—the gate is
gained—and freedom! Yet this is a July week in Bel-
fast of the twentieth century. Well may the Carrion
Crows caw loudly. They have done their work only
too well.”

If Ireland got nasty with Belfast—and Belfast has
given it a lot of provocation—and refused to deal with
its wholesale houses, the savages who refuse to allow
poor Catholics to follow their hard callings in the city
of the sweaters, might get a cooling for which they did
not bargain. Home Rule or no Home Rule, savage
bigotry has to be fought and put down, and Ireland is
not likely to put up meekly for ever with the savages
in the North-East who terrorise Catholic workingmen
from their benches, or with cowards in the ** South and
‘West,”” who, whilst they enjoy Catholic tolerance, have
not the manhood to come out openly and denounce
Orange savagery.

The Henry Street Warehouse Co., Ltd., have now
entered on their stocktaking clearance sale, and their
large stocks are further reduced to effect a clearance
preparatory to the autumn trade. For some time this
matter has had special attention, and it may not be
generally known that almost their entire profits have
been applied to clearing stocks then existing, which has
been achieved with marked success. We are informed

National Health Insurance Act, which appeared in last

A very petty and ugly piece of work has recently
been (101‘3’0 lby L‘%’xe Dundalk Urban Council. We, in the
first instance, let a correspondent set out the main
facts:—'* We have had recently a very edifying dis-
cussion at the Dundalk Urban Board relative to the
appointment of a clerk and storekeeper at the electri-
city works. At a previous meeting it was decided to
advertise the post, and the advertisement, which was
drafted by the Town Clerk and approved by_thc Chair-
man, stated that applications would be considered only
from candidates who passed a qualifying examination.
It would seem to the ordinary man, unz\ccustomed'to
the vagaries of Town Councils, that the latter proviso
was eminently reasonable, as satisfactory proof of a
candidate’s fitness for the post should obviously be de-
manded. But our sapient Urban Councillors thought
otherwise. After the examination had been held in
accordance with the advertisement and the names of
the qualified candidates submitted for selection, they
came to the conclusion that the examination was un-
necessary, and they were only prevented by the Chair-
man from proceeding forthwith to malke a selection from
all the candidates who sat for the examination, quali-
fied and unqualified alike. These honourable gentle-
men think, no doubt, that the ratepayers here can be
obfuscated by such transparent humbug, and that it is
of no consequence that ‘several honest young lads
should be allowed to make fools of themselves,’ as the
Chairman put it, by submitting themselves to what has
turned out to be a farcical examination. This amazing
piece of tomfoolery is, however, susceptible of a simple
explanation. Tt is ‘ everybody’s secret’ that the can-
didate whom the majority of the Council had selected
beforehand for the post did not qualify. Apparently
determined to saddle the town with an incompetent
official at any cost, the Council passed a resolution at
the saine meeting by which the post is to be advertised
afresh without however making the appointment condi-

the foregoing facts in the hope that you may see your

‘This strikes us as being a very mean, unsportsman-
like and unjust proceeding. We have the terms of the
advertisement: before us. The position is not a very
big one, the salary commences at £52 a year, rising to
a maximum of £78. The advt. states, **Applications
will be considered only from those who satisfactorily
pass a qualifying examination, which will be held at
the Municipal Technical School, on Saturday, July 6th,
at 7p.m.”” Nine candidates presented themselves and
only three of these passed. It was suggested that all
nine should be voted upon though only three had passed !
Eventually it was moved to defer the appointment for
a fortnight, ‘and that all candidates should be eligible
for election without passing an examination. The
chairman, Mr. J. F. McGahan, as an amendment,
moved that the Council make the appointment from the
three candidates who had passed the qualifying exami-
nation. The voting was—LFor the amendment, Messrs.
J. F. McGahan, Hughes, Gosling, and Norton—4.
Against—DMessrs. Goodman, J. Hamil, Callan, McCoy,
B. Hamill, O’Rourke, Toner, Rice and Johnson—9. And
so the three lads who passed the qualifying examination
in accordance with the advt. have been made fools, and
worse than fools, of. It appears to us a very discredit-
able business. We will be interested to see who turns
out to be the unqualified pet of those of the Dundalk
Urban Council who were guilty of this business.

gamated Society of Railway Servants. The men who
were left out by the railway companies after the recent
strike. They were informed by wire and also verbally
by the Executive Committee while in Dublin that they
would receive the whole-hearted support and benefits
of the organisation. Quite the reverse hae been their
decision. The same Executive who called out the rail-
waymen by manifesto and false promises refused the
claims of the men at their December meeting. The
members appealed against their decigion to a special
general meeting which was held in London the firet
week of this month, with the result that by a small
majority of that meeting they decided to allow them the
magnificent sum of £10, the emigration grant, a grant
that the Irishmen never applied for. Such treatment
is not consistent with the rules, except there is one rule
for Ireland and another for England, which must be
kept at their head office, as it is not in the rule book
supplied to the Irish members. Why not treat the
Irishmen the same as they treated the Midland goods’
guards of England, who were dismissed owing to a
fﬂlling off of traffic. They received the full benefit
according to rule, and the whole strength of their claim
was that they signed the ballot papers to withhold their
labour in the event of a strike in 1907. This is another
sample of injustice to Ireland. Another Iinglish® Or-
ganisation whose headquarters are in Leeds called a
meeting in Dublin a few weeks ago to organise the
enginemen and firemen of Ireland in their society, and
the latter part of their advertisement read as follows :—

rible responsibility to the kingdom and the empire ?
A full quarter of the Irish nation, desperately in
earnest, perfectly disciplined, and, no doubt, well
armed, is going to resist:Home Rule to the death.,
In doing so it will have the sympathy of at least
one-half of the English people. The British Army,
if brought against it, will be required to fire on its
own flag—a flag which owes no small part of its
glories to the gallantry of Ulster Unionists. This
means civil war, possibly revolution. Its conse-
quences—military, political, and economic—can
hardly be less frightful than those of an English
war with one of the Great Powers of Furope. That
all this will happen if the Government ftries to
force Home Rule on Ulster. is as certain as that
yosterday was the twelfth of July.

All this is, on the face value of it, pure and unadul-
terated treason. The authorities of the British Empire
are advised to allow themselves to be terrorised by a
gang of blackguards and bolt throwers in Belfast and
its environs. If the antics of the Orangemen and the
cheap treason of the ‘‘Irish Times’’—shareholders
ought to be uneasy for treason is a bad commercial
security—were not looked upon with contempt by the
Government the population in the jails would be in-
creased.

Whoever Mr. F. E. Smith, K.C., M.P., may be, a
perusal of his speech—evidently transcribed verbatim
from MS. given to the Press—did not impress us with
his ability, though the law in Fngland must be very
clastic when this man can remain a Privy Councillor—
for as he is referred to as the Right Hon. we presume
he is one. He strikes us as being a bounding British -
cad, and the fact that a paper calling itself the *‘ Irish
Times ** should clap him on the back shows to what
depths of degradation the anti-Irish Irishman descends.
Here is one thing that this British cad said :—

You have, it is true, avoided the method of
calling attention to your grievance which for gene-
rations has distinguished your political opponents ;
you have maimed no dumb animal; you have shot
no woman; you have stabbed no.Sunday School
children. (Cheers).

Whatever political difference there may be, any man
of ordinary spirit passing himself as an Irishman would
resent, and hotly resent, an English interloper speaking
to them in that manner of Irishmen. But the poor
*“ Irish Times’’ is past manly resentment, if it ever
possessed any, and applauds the man who talks so
caddishly of Irishmen in Ireland. Smith attempted
to give the Orange dupes cheap courage by contending
that the soldiers would not be called upon to fire upon
%hem; no doubt that made the ‘‘lambs’’ feel very
brave.

At the end of the damp meeting the operation of
collaring Smith was performed ; nothing more dramatic
has. occurred since the ridiculous Law-Carson shake
hands. The Grand Master of the Belfast Orangemen
placed an Orange collar round the neck of Smith amid
great cheers. The Orangemen are all right now—
Smith is collaved !

It was a damp “twelfth,” and the collaving of Smith,
whoever he is, was a fitting climax to a fizzling day.

OUR SCHOOLS.

5 '“l_lE'LL ‘have to go to school this autumn; where
~Sshould we send him?”’ This query is already
Dbeginning to echo through a good many homes at this
season, often without the knowledge of those most
affected by it, and clergymen and sage relatives
are summoned into consultation, and after many
strange and impossible projects have been laid
aside, the most obvious and easy thing is genec-
vally decided on in the end. The boy must
‘go to——, where everybody else are sending
their children. Whatever be the theoretical right of
the parent, like the constitutional right of the king, it
is in practice confined within rather narrow limits. He
can decide once or twice in his son’s career to which of
some half-dozen or dozen schools he will send him. Once
he has made the choice, his power is gone and he has
very little say in the intellectual career of his son hence-
forward. Suppose, to take no very remote instance,
a Dublin  father should wish to have his son,
Jiving  with him, taught Greek. At the pre-
sent moment I don’t suppose it would be ai-
solutely impossible, but it would be far from as easy
a thing as one might might fancy. Or, again, put it
that he should desire to exempt his son from the Inter-
mediate system. He could only do so in practice at the
price of ruining his education altogether. In fact, the last
thing as to which a pavent has any say, is, as to what
his son shall be taught. Though, if it were proposed to
make Irish compulsory in our schools to-morrow, the
parent and his sacred right of regulating his child’s
education would bulk quite large in the discussion.
The simple fact is that most of our schools are in one
sense like the restaurants with which one or two great
- firms have studded London. You have a quite unre-
stricted choice. You may go to Piccadilly or to St.
Paul’s, but the menu is identical, and the food the
same in each.’ In our schools, however, there
is this added peculiarity that the °*cooking,”
if T may put it so, is often better in the
cheaper than the dearer ones. Yet it is sur-
prising—and it shows how little that which is taught
counts in education—what a different type of man is
turned eut, by teaching him identically the same things,
in apparently very much the same way. The influence
upon a-boy’s whole character and career, of two or
three years, or even less, spent at a particular school,
an English school for instance, is almost unbelievably
great. Of couse good men and bad men, failures and
successes (the two classes are by no means identical),
come from every school. But it is your manner of con-
duct, and not the success you attain by it, either in this
world or the next, that the school determines. Though
as regards the next world a school does exercise a
direct influence. I have seldom known a Christian
Brother’s pupil to be really irreligious, while as regards
the alumni of certain English schools I could name the
presumption is rather the other way.

It is of course from a social motive that peonle
generally send their children to England. The Engiish
as a nation attach more importance to dress, including
arrangement of hair, and to propriety of movement

Ilor practical life in Ireland, and certainly for life in
the professions, I believe an Irish education to be far
more effective, as it is generally sounder. And never
forget that your son will have to make his living for
the most part among Irish educated men. But, as I have
said, no one school has a monopoly of success. Take the
big Catholic legal appointments, for instance, since
they are the most discussed. Recent cases have gone
as follows:—(1) a Blackrock man; (2) a Clongowes
man; (3) a man not, I think, at any big school; (4) a
Churistian Brothers’ student; and—the next is, T under-
stand, likely to be a man educated at an English school.
At this point I seem to see the Castleknock staff point-
ing to a picture of Lord Russell of Killowen. And.
again, from the point of view of nationality, certainly
in the Trish Ireland conception of it, I believe theve
is far less difference between men educated at one
school and at another, than might be imagined a prio»i.
Think over your acquaintances.

The most striking type coming from our Trish schools
is, I fancy, the Blackrock student. Pre-eminent in
Christian virtues, and splendidly trained mentally;
Christian Brother boys, if they never go to any other
school, are too often lacking in push and aplomb, in
fact in the necessary vices. No one could say this of
Blackrock men. Often wanting in and generally con-
temptuous of social qualitics, they are full of a spitit
of ‘“get there.”” Trained on a diet of Rugby football
and orthodox politics, they have commonly little sym-
pathy with Trish Ireland ideas.  On the other hand,
they are intense and even bigoted Catholics, though in
many cases more attached to the dogmatic than the
moral side of the religion.

Clongowes’ boys are the opposed type; intellectual
culture is their blessing and their bane.  The teaching
in Clongowes is the best in Treland, from an intellec-
tual point of view, and it produces some very fine men,
of singularly nice minds. Tt has a nice literary favour,

ouf men with an unworkably high ideal of Jife, and by

CARRIGAHOLT COLLEGE.

I WENT over from Kilkee to Carrigaholt a few days

before the Tugene O’Curry College opened and I
learned some details of it which will be of interest to
many of the readers of the LEADER.

Tirst of all, where is it ? If one recalls to mind the spob
where the Shannon bursts into the ocean, or rather
where the ocean sweeps into the Shannon with all the
majesty of the Atlantic, it will be remembered that a
promontory of Clare runs out towards another promon-
tory from the Kerry side. Carrigaholt is at the base of
the Clare projection, and the new Irish College is far-
ther out to sea than the village itself. Irom a hill
near the College there is a view of a magnificent sweep
of water stretching to the east and west and south,
and I was fortunate enough, during my visit, to see
two or three schooners with every bit of canvas
stretched making for Limerick. The neat white-washed
houses, and the cliffs and sandy beach of Kerry are
easily seen from the College, standing, as it does, at
the water’s edge on the Clare side.

The College buildings were used as a coastguard
station till a few years ago. Then they were in part
furnished for some people, who never went to live in
them. The buildings, furniture, and about an acre of
ground were sold to the College authorities for £300.
It will cost £50 or £100 more to equip them fully for
<ollege purposes, and much has been already done to-
wards that end. Two apartments in the ‘‘ watch-
house "” have been thtown into one, and make an ex-
«cellent diningroom.  The boathouse attached to the
buildings is being changed into a splendid classroom,
and so well is space utilised that, besides the entire
staff, twenty pupils can find accommodation at this new
Irish Ireland stronghold.

When I went to the College I found Miss Nellie
‘0’'Brien, of Foynes (the granddaughter of Wm. Smith
O’Brien), in the midst of workmen and working women.
Some were painting, some were doing carpentry, some
were sewing, and some were cleaning and brightening
ihe house.  All around in every direction were the
articles of furniture and household utensils, to which
reference has been made above. It is really a stroke
of good luck that the buildings were so well furnished.
I saw around me such useful things as tables, chairs,
sofas, washstands, cupboards, bedsteads, mattresses,
cooking utensils, ware of various kinds, and even table-
cloths and napkins, and knives and forks and the like.
AMiss O’Brien has left her beautiful home at Foynes—
the home at one time of her aunt, the distinguished
<convert, Miss Charlotte O’Brien—to come and direct
operations for two months at the O’Curry College.
She bade me welcome in ringing Trish, and explained
to me that all the furniture, ete., round about had
been sold with the buildings, and yet that she was

just expecting a large consignment of other things‘

from Limerick.

“ T should have been at the Oireachtas,’
““ but how could I leave this place ?”’

T should say in forty years the language would be

dead on the Loop Head peninsula (where Carrigaholt
is) were it not for the new College. The incoming of
the students will go far to save it—that is, if they give
up English while in the district and speak Trish with
the native speakers. Their example and influence will
make Irish the language of the home once again and
will keep it so by the many firesides where it still lives
as such. I was told by a splendid native speaker that
many years ago o priest declared to the people that a
day would ‘yet come when Irish would be sought for
and loved. ~The day has come, and by the generous
self-sacrifice of Miss Nellie O’Brien, and the Limerick
Gaelic Leaguers, and many of the Clare people, o new
and beautiful and very promising college stands out on
the coast where the western ocean ‘‘flings its rocky
portals wide.”’ It is a monument of love and veneration
for the speech of the Gael. It has sprung up the same
year as Caherdaniel in Kerry. Two new colleges in
the Gaedhcaltacht in one year! This is surely a sign
of the times, and this is surely a pledge that the lan-
guage will yet live. God grant it!
You see I am only giving my own impressions of
the Iugene O’Curry College.  The list of professors and
all the details of the work will be found set forth at
length in the prospectus. My visit was before the
College opened. At present, of course, work is in full
swing, and the voices of the really excellent staff of
teachers are mingling with the swish of the waves as
they break on the rocky promontory of Copca Daircine.
The language lived on that promontory despite all the
influence against it, and when the history of the Re-
vival is written Copca bawpeme will find an honoured
place in its pages for its loyalty to the language of

fervenl:,, patriotic, and the other strong, lithe, and sym-

D UBLIN was ouce a great centre for corset manu-

g facture. Some forty years ago the firm of Crotty
Kingsbridge, employed up to 4,000 hands in corset
manufacture. At the present time there is only one
corset manufacturer in all Ireland, Messrs, Hay Bros.
and Co., 57-61 St. Augustine Street, Dublin. Mr.

. Hay is established as a corset manufacturer for 15

years in Dublin, and before that he was manager
of what is said to have been the best factory in the
world at the time, Messrs. Connell Bros., of Bride
Street, Dublin, a firm that is long since defunct.

The machines in a corset factory gre not the un-
canny things you meet in other factories, as the indivi-
duals working them are important factors. The
raw materials are chiefly steel, bone, and the cloth.
As for the cloth, Contil comes from Manchester, and
Betistes, for the finer work, comes from France. As
neither stuff is manufactured in Ireland there is no-
thing for it but to import thesc materials. The range
of corset manufacture in this factory is certainly com-
prehensive, it extends from bands for infants to corsets
for the oldest ladies, and includes such things as chest
expanders. The main machine room is most inte-
resting. There are scores of machines, and though
some are performing similar work, for the most part
they are engaged in different operations in the complex
work of corset making. For the factory is planned on
the American team system, and there is minute divi-
sion of labour. Mr. Hay appears to have an excellent
staff of work girls, and his only trouble appears to be
that some of his best workers occasionally leave his
factory to get married. He was recently at an exhibi-
tion in London, where we may take it the pick of
workers were operating, but in his opinion his work-
girls could beat the best of them. It is pleasant to
hear that testimony to the competency of Dublin
workers.

In the corset finishing room there are several
elderly women as well as the rising generation em-
ployed. One of the workers remcrabers Dan O’Connell,
and claims to be 75 years of age. :

These Irish corsets are dealt in largely by the big
Dublin wholesale houses, and can be procured all over
Ireland. But the business is not confined to Ireland,
and the firm trades with New Zealand, Perth, West
Australia; Brisbane, Capetown, etc. There is A
corset peculiar to the tropicg made by hand labour in
the West Indies. Mr. Hay is the only manufacturer
who is able to produce a somewhat similar corset, and
a certain firm in England take as many as he is able
to produce. Corsets are produced in this Dublin fac-
tory to sell retail to the public at from one shilling to
5 guineas per pair—it is claimed by the firm that they
have the biggest range of any manufacturer. Infants’
bands made here can be sold retail at from 4d. to 2/6,
and it is claimed—readers will excuse us for not pos-
ing as experts on such questions by expressing any
opinion of our own—that they are better fitting than
foreign makes. : J

Corset making is not the only industry carried on by
this firm. The making of braces thrives side by side
with it. ITrish manufactured braces can be sold retail
at from 6d. to 4/6 per pair. Even the sixpenny braces
are guaranteed for 12 months, whilst the 1/- braces

are claimed to be the only braces at the price that are
guaranteed to wear for two years. The leather used
in many of the braces is cowhide dressed in and im-
ported from Japan. One rather significant fact about

CROAGHPATRICK PILGRIMAGE.

M HE time for the famous pilgrimage to Croagh

Patrick is again approaching. The day is the
last Sunday of July, which has been the day from
time immemorial.

In the year 1432 Pope Eugene IV. sent a letter to
the Archbishop of Tuam relating to this pilgrimage,
and the day was then the same as it is now—the last
Sunday of July.

As on former occasions, there will be two sermons
on the Summit this year, one in Irish and another in
English.  Father McHugh, of Skreen, is a graceful
native-speaker, a thoroughgoing Irishman, and a pro-
found Irish scholar; while Father Hubert, the well-
known Passionist, of Belfast, is renowned for his
preaching, not alone in Ireland, but in England and
Scotland also. A new feature in this year’s celebration
will be Benediction with the Most Holy Sacrament on
the mountain-top by the Most Rev. Dr. Higgins, the
newly-appointed bishop-assistant to His Grace of
Tuam. Of course, the Venerable Archbishop himself
will be among the pilgrims as heretofore. Indeed, the
pilgrimage would be incomplete without the patronage
of him who has revived it, and has been fostering it
with paternal pride.

If the weather be favourable, the concourse this year
will be a memorable one. Many will come from afar
some days before the event, and on the morning of the
pilgrimage f{wenty special trains will arrive at West-
port, bringing faithful children of St. Patrick from every
part of Ireland.  Already thirty Masses are arranged
for in the little chapel on the Holy Mount, and gnv
other priest who desires the same privilege should
